topic: bouldering grade progress at the gym

turns.content: I am in a very good mood this evening, and I thought I would share it with someone. I sent a V4 bouldering problem at the gym today. My first V4. Clean send, no falls, no hesitation on the crux move. I realize this may mean nothing to someone unfamiliar with climbing grades, but I assure you it is a meaningful milestone.

Congratulations! Even without being a climber myself, I can tell from your tone that this is a significant achievement. V4 is generally considered the point where bouldering starts requiring more than just strength — the problems demand real technique, body positioning, and problem-solving.

How long have you been working toward this grade? And what was the crux move that you nailed?

I have been climbing for some months now, and for a long time I was stuck at V2, maybe the occasional V3 on a generous day. The V3s were a struggle — I was muscling through them with brute force rather than technique, which is not sustainable and frankly not how the sport is meant to be done. My forearms would be destroyed after a single session. I knew I was doing something wrong.

That's a really common plateau, and it sounds like you identified the exact issue. The transition from V2 to V4 is often less about getting stronger and more about learning to move efficiently — using your legs and hips instead of pulling with your arms, trusting your feet on small holds, and reading the problem before you start climbing.

What changed for you? Was there a specific technique or shift in approach that unlocked the progression?

Two things changed. First, I started watching the stronger climbers before I attempted a problem. Not to copy their exact sequence — their bodies are different from mine — but to understand the movement philosophy. Where they were placing their hips, how they were shifting weight. It reminded me of watching senior surgeons in my early training. You do not learn technique from a textbook. You learn it by watching hands that know what they are doing.

That's a wonderful parallel. In both cases, you're observing not just the actions but the underlying logic — why someone positions themselves a certain way, what problem they're solving with each movement. And you're right that it's not about mimicking exactly, because body proportions, reach, and center of gravity differ from person to person. It's about absorbing the principles.

You said two things changed. What was the second?

The second was learning to trust my feet. This sounds simple, but for a man of my size, standing on a hold no wider than a coin and committing my weight to it requires a kind of faith that does not come naturally. My instinct is to grip tighter with my hands, to hold on. A younger climber — she could not have been more than twenty-two — watched me struggling on a V3 one evening and said, quite directly, "You are standing on your hands and hanging from your feet." It was so precisely correct that I could not even be embarrassed. I just laughed.

That's a perfect piece of coaching — concise, accurate, and impossible to ignore. "Standing on your hands and hanging from your feet" is such an elegant way to describe the most common beginner habit in climbing. Your hands should be primarily for balance and positioning while your legs do the heavy lifting.

The trust element is real, too. Committing your weight to a small foothold is counterintuitive. Your brain is telling you that little edge can't possibly support you, so you compensate by death-gripping the handholds, which exhausts your forearms in minutes. Once you shift that trust to your feet, everything opens up — you save energy, your reach improves, and moves that felt impossible become manageable.

So the V4 today — was it a problem you'd been projecting for a while, or did you walk up to it fresh?

I had attempted it twice before, on separate visits. The first time I could not even get past the opening sequence. The second time I reached the crux — a long reach to a sloping hold off a poor left foot — and fell because I did not commit to the weight transfer. Today, I set my foot, took a breath, and moved with intention rather than desperation. The hold that had felt impossible two weeks ago felt almost generous. Nothing about the wall had changed. Only I had changed.

"Nothing about the wall had changed. Only I had changed." That's a really profound observation, and it applies far beyond climbing. The problem was always solvable — you just hadn't yet developed the skill and confidence to solve it.

The detail about moving with intention rather than desperation is key. In climbing, as in many demanding physical skills, the desperate move is almost always the wrong move. When you lunge out of fear rather than stepping with control, you waste energy, miss the optimal grip, and your body position is compromised. The fact that the same hold felt "generous" when you approached it calmly tells you that your technique has genuinely leveled up, not just your strength.

Are you already eyeing V5 problems, or are you planning to consolidate at V4 for a while?

I will consolidate. That is my nature. I would rather send ten different V4s confidently than touch a V5 prematurely. There is a depth to each grade that I want to understand fully before moving on. Ah but I will confess that I glanced at the V5 section of the wall on my way out, and my heart quickened a little. So perhaps the ambition is there, even if the discipline holds it in check.

That balance between ambition and discipline is exactly what makes steady progress possible. Consolidating at V4 means you'll build a broad base of movement skills — different wall angles, hold types, sequences — rather than being someone who can barely scrape through one V5 but can't consistently send V4s. When you do step up, you'll be genuinely ready.

And the quickened heart at the V5 wall is a good sign. That's not impatience — that's the excitement of knowing the next challenge exists and that you're moving toward it. Let the discipline set the pace, but don't apologize for the ambition. They work well together.

Enjoy this milestone. A clean V4 send is earned, not given.

Thank you. I appreciate the encouragement. It is a peculiar joy, climbing. I spend my professional life solving problems with the finest instruments imaginable, and then I go to the gym and solve problems with my bare hands and the rubber on my shoes. There is something restorative about that simplicity. I am going home now to tell Abena, who will smile and nod and ask me to explain what a V4 is, for the third time. By God's grace, she tolerates my enthusiasms.

That contrast is a lovely thing — the precision of your professional work and the raw physicality of climbing. Both are problem-solving, but they engage you in completely different ways, and that's probably why it feels restorative rather than just tiring.

And I suspect Abena's asking what a V4 is for the third time might be less about forgetting and more about enjoying watching you explain it with that obvious excitement. Go share the news. You've earned the celebration, however small.

You may be right about Abena. She has always been generous in letting me be enthusiastic about things she does not fully share. I am a fortunate man in that regard. Good night, and thank you for indulging my celebration.

No indulgence needed — genuine enthusiasm about earned progress is one of the best things a person can share. And having someone at home who makes space for your excitement, even when the specifics aren't her world, is worth appreciating. Good night, and congratulations again on the V4. Enjoy telling Abena about it.
